name: Hassaniya Arabic (Mauritanian)
notes: Ḥassāniyya Arabic (klām əl-Bīđ̣ān), the Bedouin Maghrebi dialect of Mauritania, Western Sahara and the borderlands of Mali/Algeria/southern Morocco, described by Taine-Cheikh (2007, EALL 'Ḥassāniyya Arabic', pp.240-250) and Cohen (1963). It is a conservative Bedouin dialect that is typologically closer to eastern Arabic than to sedentary Maghrebi. DEFINING FEATURES: (1) qāf ق → [ɡ] (Taine-Cheikh §2.1.1.2 'the realization of qāf as [g]'); [q] survives in Classical borrowings and, for most speakers, as the merger reflex of ġayn — /ġ/ and /q/ merge to [q] outside the west, and always under gemination (§2.1.1.1). (2) THE INTERDENTALS ARE RETAINED: ث [θ], ذ [ð], and ḍād ض → đ̣ [ðˤ] 'the reflex of most words with ḍād' (§2.1.1.2), merging with ẓāʾ ظ [ðˤ]. (3) THE ḤASSĀNIYYA /v/: the labial spirant *f is realised PREFERENTIALLY AS VOICED [v] — 'except in contact with a voiceless consonant or when it is geminate' (§2.1.1.3); that voiceless-context devoicing back to [f] is conditioned by an adjacent consonant and is not expressed as a rule here (the transcription keeps [v] there — a documented under-generation). This voiced reflex, written ⟨ڤ⟩, is 'particular to this dialect' and is its most famous consonantal trait (dvəl 'to spit', šāvu 'they saw'). (4) ǧīm ج → [ʒ] (§2.1.1.3 '/j/ is realized as a palato-alveolar fricative'). (5) RICH EMPHATIC SYSTEM: Ḥassāniyya phonemicises emphasis widely — /ṛ/ [rˤ] (more frequent than plain /r/), /ḷ/ [lˤ], and /ẓ/ [zˤ] borrowed from Zenaga Berber, alongside /tˤ dˤ sˤ ðˤ/ and marginal velarised /ḅ ṃ ṇ ṿ/ — with minimal pairs like walla 'to come back' vs waḷḷa 'or', dār 'to put' vs dāṛ 'to want' (§2.1.1.1). These emphatic sonorants are lexical, not predictable from the abjad, so they are documented here but emitted only through the inherited emphasis backing of /a aː/ → [ɑ ɑː]. VOWELS (§2.1.2): long /iː uː aː/, short /a ə/, with /i/ and /u/ merged to /ə/ in closed syllables (a nomadic trait, Cohen 1970); /a/ undergoes ʔimāla to a centralized [æ] (žä 'he came'). DIPHTHONGS (§2.1.3): the four Classical diphthongs /aj aw ij uw/ are PRESERVED, with /aj aw/ variably tending to [eː oː]. Berber (Zenaga) substrate and Black-African (Wolof/Fula/Soninke) contact add marginal phonemes (palatalised /dy ty ny/, /ŋ/).

sources.author: Taine-Cheikh, C.
sources.title: Ḥassāniyya Arabic (Encyclopedia of Arabic Language and Linguistics, vol. 2, Eg-Lan)
sources.notes: Read (open-access HAL author manuscript). §2.1: qāf → [g] and interdental retention (§2.1.1.2); labial spirant → voiced [v] except before/after a voiceless consonant or when geminate (§2.1.1.3); ǧīm → [ʒ]; the emphatic system incl. /ṛ ḷ ẓ/ with minimal pairs (§2.1.1.1); vowels /iː uː aː/ + /a ə/, i/u merger in closed syllables, ʔimāla (§2.1.2); the four diphthongs preserved (§2.1.3).

sources.author: Cohen, D. & el Chennafi, M.
sources.title: Le dialecte arabe ḥassānīya de Mauritanie (parler de la Gǝbla)
sources.notes: The reference grammar of Ḥassāniyya. Cited bibliographically; not obtained to a readable page for this pass.

sources.author: Heath, J.
sources.title: Hassaniya Arabic (Mali)–English–French Dictionary
sources.notes: Grammar introduction to the Malian Ḥassāniyya variety; documents the same [v]/qāf-[g]/retained-interdental profile. Cited bibliographically.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: General Arabic phonology: emphasis and its backing of /a/ → [ɑ]; *q → [ɡ] in Bedouin dialects.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
